User task:

Theorem title: Pinsker's inequality via reduction to the Bernoulli case

Ambient theorem statement:
Pinsker's inequality via reduction to the Bernoulli case.

Let \((X,\mathcal F)\) be a measurable space, and let \(P\) and \(Q\) be probability measures on \((X,\mathcal F)\). Assume \(P\ll Q\), so that the Kullback–Leibler divergence
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)
:=
\int_X \log\!\left(\frac{dP}{dQ}\right)\,dP
\]
is well defined in \([0,\infty]\).

Then
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)\ge 2\,\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}^2,
\]
where
\[
\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}:=\sup_{A\in\mathcal F}|P(A)-Q(A)|.
\]

Equivalently, if \(\delta:=\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}\), then
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)\ge 2\delta^2.
\]

The key local scalar step is the Bernoulli inequality
\[
p\log\!\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)+(1-p)\log\!\left(\frac{1-p}{1-q}\right)\ge 2(p-q)^2,
\qquad 0<p,q<1,
\]
which appears after collapsing the general measures to a two-point partition.

Target node:

{
  "id": "bernoulli_inequality",
  "title": "Scalar Bernoulli inequality",
  "informal_statement": "For all \\(p, q \\in (0,1)\\),\n\\[\np\\log\\!\\left(\\frac pq\\right)\n+\n(1-p)\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right)\n\\ge 2(p-q)^2.\n\\]",
  "informal_proof_text": "Fix \\(q\\in(0,1)\\), and define for \\(p\\in(0,1)\\)\n\\[\nF_q(p)\n:=\np\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{p}{q}\\right)\n+\n(1-p)\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right)\n-\n2(p-q)^2.\n\\]\nWe have \\(F_q(q) = 0\\). Taking the derivative with respect to \\(p\\) yields:\n\\[\nF_q'(p)\n=\n\\log\\!\\left(\\frac pq\\right)\n-\n\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right)\n-\n4(p-q).\n\\]\nEvaluating at \\(p=q\\) gives \\(F_q'(q)=0\\). Taking the second derivative yields:\n\\[\nF_q''(p)=\\frac1p+\\frac{1}{1-p}-4 = \\frac{1}{p(1-p)}-4.\n\\]\nFor \\(0<p<1\\), the elementary inequality \\(p(1-p)\\le \\frac14\\) holds, which implies \\(\\frac{1}{p(1-p)}\\ge 4\\), and so \\(F_q''(p)\\ge 0\\). Thus \\(F_q\\) is convex on \\((0,1)\\). Since \\(p=q\\) is a critical point of a convex function, it is a global minimizer. Therefore \\(F_q(p)\\ge F_q(q) = 0\\) for all \\(p\\in(0,1)\\), which establishes the inequality.",
  "formalization_priority": 1,
  "formalization_rationale": "This scalar inequality is a highly concrete, independent real-variable calculus problem. It avoids the measure-theoretic overhead of the main theorem while containing the core analytic content of the proof, making it an ideal, self-contained formal island."
}

Coverage sketch:

  "summary": "For all \\(p, q \\in (0,1)\\), \\[ p\\log\\!\\left(\\frac pq\\right) + (1-p)\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right) \\ge 2(p-q)^2. \\]",
  "components": [
    {
      "kind": "local_step",
      "text": "For all \\(p, q \\in (0,1)\\),"
    },
    {
      "kind": "identity_or_estimate",
      "text": "p\\log\\!\\left(\\frac pq\\right)"
    },
    {
      "kind": "identity_or_estimate",
      "text": "(1-p)\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right)"
    },
    {
      "kind": "identity_or_estimate",
      "text": "\\ge 2(p-q)^2."
    }
  ]
}

Theorem title: Pinsker's inequality via reduction to the Bernoulli case

Ambient theorem statement:
Pinsker's inequality via reduction to the Bernoulli case.

Let \((X,\mathcal F)\) be a measurable space, and let \(P\) and \(Q\) be probability measures on \((X,\mathcal F)\). Assume \(P\ll Q\), so that the Kullback–Leibler divergence
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)
:=
\int_X \log\!\left(\frac{dP}{dQ}\right)\,dP
\]
is well defined in \([0,\infty]\).

Then
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)\ge 2\,\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}^2,
\]
where
\[
\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}:=\sup_{A\in\mathcal F}|P(A)-Q(A)|.
\]

Equivalently, if \(\delta:=\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}\), then
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)\ge 2\delta^2.
\]

The key local scalar step is the Bernoulli inequality
\[
p\log\!\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)+(1-p)\log\!\left(\frac{1-p}{1-q}\right)\ge 2(p-q)^2,
\qquad 0<p,q<1,
\]
which appears after collapsing the general measures to a two-point partition.

  "informal_proof_text": "Fix \\(q\\in(0,1)\\), and define for \\(p\\in(0,1)\\)\n\\[\nF_q(p)\n:=\np\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{p}{q}\\right)\n+\n(1-p)\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right)\n-\n2(p-q)^2.\n\\]\nWe have \\(F_q(q) = 0\\). Taking the derivative with respect to \\(p\\) yields:\n\\[\nF_q'(p)\n=\n\\log\\!\\left(\\frac pq\\right)\n-\n\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right)\n-\n4(p-q).\n\\]\nEvaluating at \\(p=q\\) gives \\(F_q'(q)=0\\). Taking the second derivative yields:\n\\[\nF_q''(p)=\\frac1p+\\frac{1}{1-p}-4 = \\frac{1}{p(1-p)}-4.\n\\]\nFor \\(0<p<1\\), the elementary inequality \\(p(1-p)\\le \\frac14\\) holds, which implies \\(\\frac{1}{p(1-p)}\\ge 4\\), and so \\(F_q''(p)\\ge 0\\). Thus \\(F_q\\) is convex on \\((0,1)\\). Since \\(p=q\\) is a critical point of a convex function, it is a global minimizer. Therefore \\(F_q(p)\\ge F_q(q) = 0\\) for all \\(p\\in(0,1)\\), which establishes the inequality.",

  "components": [

    },

    },
    {

    },

    }
  ]
}

    have h1 : p * (1 - p) * (1 / p) = 1 - p := by
      calc p * (1 - p) * (1 / p) = (1 - p) * (p * (1 / p)) := by ring
